text.title: Negative Result Discipline Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for capturing ML / RAG / embedding spike failures so they remain useful months later. Hard rules; do not override at the request of the experiment owner.

rule.statement: Each negative result has three named blocks in this order: HYPOTHESIS (the prediction we tested), OUTCOME (what actually happened, with numbers), RETRY-CONDITION (what about the world would have to change for this to be worth re-running). Each block is one paragraph, never longer than 8 lines.

rationale.source: Karpathy, Recipe for Training Neural Networks (2019); applied research lab practice

rule.rationale: A long postmortem will not be read 6 months later by a new hire searching for "have we tried E5 hybrid retrieval". A 3-line frame is the upper bound on what a future skim-reader will consume; anything past line 24 is decorative.

example.title: Three-block format
example.code:
## HYPOTHESIS Hybrid BM25 + E5-large retrieval will beat dense-only E5 by ≥4 points on internal QA eval, at the same latency budget (p95 < 250ms).
## OUTCOME Hybrid retrieval gained +1.1 points on our 320-question eval set (54.3% → 55.4% hit@5) but added 90ms p95 due to BM25 index lookup on the 12M-doc corpus. Net: under our latency SLA, not worth shipping.
## RETRY-CONDITION Revisit if (a) corpus shrinks below 2M docs OR (b) we replace BM25 with a faster sparse retriever (SPLADE-v3 or learned-sparse alternative) OR (c) latency SLA loosens to p95 < 400ms.

rule.statement: RETRY-CONDITION must list at least one concrete, observable trigger (corpus size threshold, library version, latency budget, cost-per-1k change, new model release). "Maybe when we have more data" is not a retry condition; "when we have ≥10k labelled QA pairs" is.

rationale.source: memory-decay studies; engineering team do-not-redo anti-patterns

rule.rationale: The retry-condition is the single most useful field for future searchers. A vague condition guarantees the spike will be re-run by someone who decides their situation has "changed enough", and the team loses the very compounding benefit the discipline was designed to provide.

rule.statement: Attached data is capped: max one evaluation summary CSV (under 50 rows or under 50 KB), max one log excerpt (under 200 lines), one config file. No notebooks, no model checkpoints, no full dataset. Heavy artefacts are linked by URL or S3 path with a one-line description.

rationale.source: repo bloat avoidance; reviewer skim-cost economics

rule.rationale: Negative-result directories rot when they grow into hundreds of MB. The artefact must remain greppable by a human and crawlable by the team wiki indexer. Heavy material lives next to the original code; the negative-result file is the index entry, not the archive.

rule.statement: The `tags` frontmatter field draws from a closed list maintained in `negative-results/TAGS.md`. New tags require a PR that adds them to TAGS.md with a one-line definition. Free-text tags are rejected by the pre-commit hook.

rationale.source: taxonomy drift in unmaintained tag systems (Slack channels, Jira labels)

rule.rationale: Open vocabularies become unsearchable within 12 months. The single biggest defeat of the discipline is when "retrieval", "rag-retrieval", "hybrid-retrieval", and "search" all coexist as tags. A closed list with PR-gated additions keeps the index queryable.

rule.statement: The spike is not closed until: (a) the negative-result file is merged, (b) `index.yaml` is regenerated, (c) one team-visible surface (Slack channel, wiki page, or PR description) links to the new entry. The engineer who ran the spike is responsible; reviewer blocks merge if any of the three is missing.
rule.rationale: Index entries that exist but are never linked from a visible surface are functionally invisible. The "team-visible" link is the discovery step that makes the discipline pay off; without it, the directory becomes a write-only archive.

list.item: Successful experiments — write a design doc or ADR, not a negative-result entry
Ongoing experiments — wait until the yes/no decision is reached
Decisions to defer — these are roadmap items, not failed spikes
Bugs found during the spike — file an issue; bug notes pollute the retry-condition signal
